content.problemStatement:
After failing to clear his school mathematics examination, infinitepro decided to prepare very hard for his upcoming re-exam, starting with the topic he is weakest at ‚Äē computational geometry.

Being an artist, infinitepro has CCC pencils (numbered 111 through CCC); each of them draws with one of CCC distinct colours. He draws NNN lines (numbered 111 through NNN) in a 2D Cartesian coordinate system; for each valid iii, the iii-th line is drawn with the cic_ici‚Äč-th pencil and it is described by the equation y=ai‚čÖx+biy = a_i \cdot x + b_iy=ai‚Äč‚čÖx+bi‚Äč.

Now, infinitepro calls a triangle truly-geometric if each of its sides is part of some line he drew and all three sides have the same colour. He wants to count these triangles, but there are too many of them! After a lot of consideration, he decided to erase a subset of the NNN lines he drew. He wants to do it with his eraser, which has length KKK.

Whenever erasing a line with a colour iii, the length of the eraser decreases by ViV_iVi‚Äč. In other words, when the eraser has length kkk and we use it to erase a line with a colour iii, the length of the eraser decreases to k‚ąíVik-V_ik‚ąíVi‚Äč; if k<Vik \lt V_ik<Vi‚Äč, it is impossible to erase such a line.

Since infinitepro has to study for the re-exam, he wants to minimise the number of truly-geometric triangles. Can you help him find the minimum possible number of truly-geometric triangles which can be obtained by erasing a subset of the NNN lines in an optimal way? He promised a grand treat for you if he passes the examination!
constraints: 0‚Č§ai,bi‚Č§1090 \le a_i, b_i \le 10^90‚Č§ai‚Äč,bi‚Äč‚Č§109 for each valid iii
1‚Č§ci‚Č§C1 \le c_i \le C1‚Č§ci‚Äč‚Č§C for each valid iii
0‚Č§Vi‚Č§K0 \le V_i \le K0‚Č§Vi‚Äč‚Č§K for each valid iii
no two lines coincide, regardless of their colours
**Subtask #3 (25 points):** no two lines are parallel
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of the input contains three space-separated integers NNN, CCC and KKK.
NNN lines follow. For each iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \le i \le N1‚Č§i‚Č§N), the iii-th of these lines contains three space-separated integers aia_iai‚Äč, bib_ibi‚Äč and cic_ici‚Äč.
The last line contains CCC space-separated integers V1,V2,‚Ä¶,VCV_1, V_2, \ldots, V_CV1‚Äč,V2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,VC‚Äč.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ‚Äē the smallest possible number of truly-geometric triangles after erasing lines.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: We can remove exactly one line. Initially, we have 555 truly geometric triangles (see the image below; red is colour 111 and green is colour 222).

Removing any line with colour 222 brings the total number of truly-geometric triangles down to 4+0=44+0=44+0=4.
Removing any line with colour 111 brings the total number of truly-geometric triangles down to 1+1=21+1=21+1=2.

Thus, the smallest number of truly-geometric triangles we can obtain is 222.

Example case 2: We can remove at most 222 lines and removing any 222 lines gives us a total of 444 truly-geometric triangles.
